quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Coulomb's law"?

options.text: Different wavelengths refract by different amounts (n varies with λ); produces spectrum separation in prisms and rainbows
F = kq₁q₂/r²; k = 8.99 × 10⁹ N·m²/C²; inversely proportional to r²; analogous to gravitational force
The total entropy of an isolated system never decreases; natural processes are irreversible
Peaks at iron-56; lighter nuclei release energy by fusion; heavier nuclei release energy by fission

quiz_questions.explanation: "Coulomb's law" is defined as: F = kq₁q₂/r²; k = 8.99 × 10⁹ N·m²/C²; inversely proportional to r²; analogous to gravitational force. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Electric field"?

options.text: E = F/q = kQ/r² for a point charge; a vector field showing the force a positive test charge would experience at each point
V(t) = V₀ sin(ωt); alternates between +V₀ and -V₀; ω = 2πf; US standard: 120V RMS at 60 Hz
F_b = ρ_fluid · V_displaced · g; an object floats when its weight equals the buoyant force (average density < fluid density)
Fission neutrons trigger more fissions; critical mass: self-sustaining; supercritical: explosive; moderated in reactors

quiz_questions.explanation: "Electric field" is defined as: E = F/q = kQ/r² for a point charge; a vector field showing the force a positive test charge would experience at each point. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Field lines"?

options.text: Light nuclei combine: ²H + ³H → ⁴He + n + 17.6 MeV; requires extreme temperatures (>10⁷ K) to overcome Coulomb barrier
ᴬ_Z X: A = mass number (p + n), Z = atomic number (protons), N = A - Z = neutrons; isotopes differ in N
f_observed = f_source · (v ± v_observer)/(v ∓ v_source); approaching gives higher pitch, receding gives lower pitch
Visual representation of E: start on + charges, end on - charges; density indicates field strength; never cross

quiz_questions.explanation: "Field lines" is defined as: Visual representation of E: start on + charges, end on - charges; density indicates field strength; never cross. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Superposition of fields"?

options.text: Net E at a point = vector sum of fields from all individual charges; requires component-wise addition
Energy needed to completely remove an electron: for hydrogen, E_ionization = 13.6 eV (from n=1 to n=∞)
Accelerate charged particles to high energies using electric fields and steer with magnetic fields; colliders probe fundamental particles
Diagnostic imaging (gamma cameras, PET) and therapy (targeted radiation, brachytherapy) using radioactive isotopes

quiz_questions.explanation: "Superposition of fields" is defined as: Net E at a point = vector sum of fields from all individual charges; requires component-wise addition. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Charge conservation"?

options.text: Series: R_total = R₁ + R₂; same current through each. Parallel: 1/R_total = 1/R₁ + 1/R₂; same voltage across each
Ground state: lowest energy (n=1); excited states: n > 1; atoms absorb energy to reach excited states, emit to return
Total charge in an isolated system remains constant; charges can transfer between objects but cannot be created or destroyed
Many slits produce very sharp bright fringes at d sin θ = mλ; used for precise wavelength measurement (spectroscopy)

quiz_questions.explanation: "Charge conservation" is defined as: Total charge in an isolated system remains constant; charges can transfer between objects but cannot be created or destroyed. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "F = kq₁q₂/r²; k = 8.99 × 10⁹ N·m²/C²; inversely proportional to r²; analogous to gravitational force"?

options.text: Quantum mechanical model
Coulomb's law
RMS values
Reversible vs. irreversible

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Coulomb's law".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "E = F/q = kQ/r² for a point charge; a vector field showing the force a positive test charge would experience at each point"?

options.text: Thin-film interference
Entropy
Electric current
Electric field

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Electric field".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Visual representation of E: start on + charges, end on - charges; density indicates field strength; never cross"?

options.text: Magnetic field of a wire
Field lines
Brewster's angle
Magnetic force on a charge

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Field lines".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Net E at a point = vector sum of fields from all individual charges; requires component-wise addition"?

options.text: Dielectric constant (κ)
Thomson's experiment
Superposition of fields
Magnetic force on a charge

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Superposition of fields".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Total charge in an isolated system remains constant; charges can transfer between objects but cannot be created or destroyed"?

options.text: Mass defect
Rydberg formula
Charge conservation
Self-inductance

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Charge conservation".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following statements about Electricity is accurate?

options.text: Electricity studies electric charges, forces, and fields.
Magnetic Fields and Forces explores the interaction between magnetic fields and charged particles.
Electric Potential relates to the potential energy per unit charge in an electric field.
Fission is the basis of nuclear reactors; fusion powers the Sun.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Electricity as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which statement correctly describes an aspect of Electricity?

options.text: Ideal Gas Behavior is described by the ideal gas law: PV = nRT (or PV = NkT), relating pressure P, volume V, temperature T, and amount of gas n (moles) or N (molecules).
Beta-plus decay (β⁺): proton → neutron + positron + neutrino (Z→Z-1).
Coulomb's law gives the electrostatic force between point charges: F = kq₁q₂/r² (attractive for opposite charges, repulsive for like charges).
The Rydberg formula for hydrogen: 1/λ = R_H(1/n_f² - 1/n_i²), where R_H = 1.097 × 10⁷ m⁻¹.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Electricity as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which is true specifically about Electricity?

options.text: The intensity and spectrum of thermal radiation depend on temperature (Wien's law: λ_max = b/T; Stefan-Boltzmann: P = σAT⁴).
The kinetic theory of gases derives macroscopic properties from molecular motion.
The electric field E = F/q is the force per unit positive test charge.
Each element has a unique spectrum.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Electricity as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best explains a key idea of Electricity?

options.text: The strong nuclear force binds nucleons together, overcoming electrostatic repulsion between protons.
Young's double-slit experiment demonstrates wave nature: constructive interference occurs at d sin θ = mλ (bright fringes, m = 0,±1,±2,...); destructive at d sin θ = (m+½)λ (dark fringes).
EMF can be induced by changing B, changing A, or changing the angle θ between B and the area vector.
Field lines point away from positive charges and toward negative charges.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Electricity as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which accurately reflects the content on Electricity?

options.text: Superposition: the net electric field from multiple charges is the vector sum of individual fields.
Beta-minus decay (β⁻): neutron → proton + electron + antineutrino (Z→Z+1).
The intensity and spectrum of thermal radiation depend on temperature (Wien's law: λ_max = b/T; Stefan-Boltzmann: P = σAT⁴).
Converging (convex) lenses are thicker in the middle and bring parallel rays to a focus; diverging (concave) lenses are thinner in the middle and spread rays apart.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Electricity as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following is a real-world application of Electricity?

options.text: Lightning: charge separation in thunderclouds creates enormous electric fields (≈3 × 10⁶ V/m) that ionize air and create conductive channels, equalizing charge via massive current discharge
LED color selection: the semiconductor band gap determines the emitted photon wavelength; engineers tune the gap to produce specific colors (red, blue, green, white)
The Sun converts 600 million tons of hydrogen into 596 million tons of helium each second; the 4 million ton mass deficit becomes 3.8 × 10²⁶ W of radiant power via E = mc²
Charged particle therapy: proton beams for cancer treatment are steered using electromagnetic forces to precisely target tumors deep in the body

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Electricity.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which example demonstrates the practical relevance of Electricity?

options.text: The Sun converts 600 million tons of hydrogen into 596 million tons of helium each second; the 4 million ton mass deficit becomes 3.8 × 10²⁶ W of radiant power via E = mc²
Xerox copiers use electrostatic attraction: a charged drum attracts toner particles to form an image, then heat fuses the toner to paper
Rainbows: sunlight refracts upon entering water droplets, reflects internally, and refracts again upon exiting—dispersion separates colors at different angles
Cameras: converging lenses focus light from objects at varying distances onto the film/sensor by adjusting the lens-to-sensor distance (focusing)

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Electricity.

quiz_questions.question_text: How is Electricity applied in real-world contexts?

options.text: The James Webb Space Telescope uses a 6.5m segmented gold-coated mirror to collect infrared light from the earliest galaxies in the universe
Electric field therapy (tumor treating fields): alternating electric fields disrupt cancer cell division—a novel application of electrostatics in medicine
Sonar uses sound pulse travel time in water to measure ocean depth and detect objects—essential for submarine navigation and marine biology research
Scuba diving: at depth, the increased pressure compresses air in the lungs (Boyle's law); divers must ascend slowly to avoid decompression sickness (the bends)

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Electricity.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which scenario illustrates the principles of Electricity?

options.text: Rainbows: sunlight refracts upon entering water droplets, reflects internally, and refracts again upon exiting—dispersion separates colors at different angles
Radio reception: AM radio waves (λ ≈ 200-600m) diffract around buildings and mountains, while FM (λ ≈ 3m) requires more line-of-sight; why AM reaches farther in hilly terrain
Lightning: charge separation in thunderclouds creates enormous electric fields (≈3 × 10⁶ V/m) that ionize air and create conductive channels, equalizing charge via massive current discharge
Neon signs: specific gases glow with characteristic colors because their unique energy level spacings produce photons at specific visible wavelengths

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Electricity.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best shows Electricity in practice?

options.text: Energy storage: supercapacitors bridge the gap between batteries and conventional capacitors, providing rapid charge/discharge for regenerative braking in electric vehicles
Xerox copiers use electrostatic attraction: a charged drum attracts toner particles to form an image, then heat fuses the toner to paper
Radio reception: AM radio waves (λ ≈ 200-600m) diffract around buildings and mountains, while FM (λ ≈ 3m) requires more line-of-sight; why AM reaches farther in hilly terrain
CD/DVD reading: a laser reflects off pits and lands with different path lengths, creating constructive and destructive interference that encodes digital data

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Electricity.
